summary: Gruber analyzes why the upcoming Verizon iPhone announcement is being hosted by Verizon in New York rather than by Apple in Cupertino. He argues Apple wants to avoid inflated expectations that an Apple-hosted event would generate, since there's no new product — just an existing phone on a new carrier. He also notes that Verizon needs the freedom to disparage AT&T, which Apple couldn't permit at its own event since it's keeping AT&T as a peer carrier. Gruber predicts Jobs won't attend, though he acknowledges reports suggesting otherwise.
key_points: Apple-hosted events create expectations of major new products, which would be mismatched with simply adding a carrier
From a consumer perspective, the CDMA iPhone 4 is the same phone on a different network — not a new product worth an Apple keynote
Apple must treat Verizon and AT&T as peers, so it can't host an event where Verizon bashes AT&T
Apple's messaging goal is to frame this as routine good news, not a seismic shift
money_quotes: Apple is fully aware that when they say 'We're having an event next week', people expect big news and a new product.
We nerds know that a CDMA iPhone 4 is a different device than the GSM iPhone 4, but from a consumer perspective, it's the same phone but just works on a different carrier.
This is the sort of thing where Apple needs to play it cool, and act like this is just another carrier. Good news, but not big news.
The bottom line from Apple is going to be something like, 'We're delighted to add Verizon as an iPhone carrier'. That's it.
Apple is not leaving AT&T for Verizon. They're adding Verizon as a peer to AT&T.
Verizon can disparage AT&T at a Verizon event. Apple couldn't let them do that at an Apple event.
key_insight: Apple let Verizon host the iPhone announcement because Apple-hosted events set product expectations too high for what's essentially a carrier deal, and because Verizon needs the freedom to compete against AT&T in ways Apple can't endorse.
